questions.q: The Supreme Court of India is described as the highest body of which branch of government?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body and the final court of appeal in India.

questions.q: On what date was the Supreme Court of India established?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950.

questions.q: Who heads the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India is the head of the Supreme Court.

questions.q: In which city is the Supreme Court of India located?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is located in New Delhi.

questions.q: The Supreme Court is often described as the guardian of which document?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the guardian and final interpreter of the Constitution.

questions.q: Who appoints the judges of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: Judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by the President of India.

questions.q: Up to what age may a judge of the Supreme Court hold office?
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge holds office until the age of 65 years.

questions.q: Which of these is one of the three jurisdictions exercised by the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court exercises original, appellate and advisory jurisdiction.

questions.q: Who was the first Chief Justice of India?
questions.explanation: H.J. Kania was the first Chief Justice of India.

questions.q: A decision of the Supreme Court is binding on which other courts in India?
questions.explanation: The law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within the territory of India.

questions.q: The Supreme Court is established under which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Union judiciary, including the Supreme Court, is dealt with in Part V of the Constitution.

questions.q: Which article of the Constitution provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court.

questions.q: Under which article does the Supreme Court issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.q: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court covers disputes between which parties?
options: The Union and States, or between States
questions.explanation: Under Article 131, the Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in disputes between the Union and States or among States.

questions.q: The advisory jurisdiction, under which the President may seek the Supreme Court's opinion on a question of law, is provided by which article?
questions.explanation: Article 143 allows the President to refer questions of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion.

questions.q: The Special Leave Petition, allowing the Supreme Court to grant leave to appeal from any court or tribunal, arises under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 136 grants the Supreme Court discretion to allow special leave to appeal from any court or tribunal.

questions.q: Who was the first woman judge appointed to the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: Justice M. Fathima Beevi became the first woman judge of the Supreme Court of India in 1989.

questions.q: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office only through which process?
options: A decision of the Bar Council
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge can be removed only by an order of the President after a special-majority address by both Houses of Parliament on grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity.

questions.q: Before retirement, the language of the Constitution requires that a person be a judge of a High Court for how many years to qualify as a Supreme Court judge by that route?
questions.explanation: One qualifying route is having been a judge of a High Court for at least five years.

questions.q: The power of the Supreme Court to review laws for constitutional validity is known as what?
questions.explanation: Judicial review allows the Supreme Court to examine and strike down laws that violate the Constitution.

questions.q: The original sanctioned strength of the Supreme Court at its inception was one Chief Justice and how many other judges?
questions.explanation: At its inception in 1950, the Supreme Court had a Chief Justice and seven other judges.

questions.q: A retired Supreme Court judge is, by convention and rule, barred from doing what?
options: Pleading or acting before any court within India
questions.explanation: A retired Supreme Court judge is prohibited from practising or pleading before any court or authority within the territory of India.

questions.q: In the 1973 Kesavananda Bharati case, the Supreme Court enunciated which doctrine limiting Parliament's amending power?
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot alter the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.q: The current sanctioned strength of the Supreme Court is one Chief Justice and how many other judges, as fixed in 2019?
questions.explanation: Since 2019, the sanctioned strength is the Chief Justice plus 33 other judges, a total of 34.

questions.q: Article 137 confers on the Supreme Court the power to do what regarding its own judgments?
options: Review its own judgments and orders
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review any judgment pronounced or order made by it.

questions.q: Who was the longest-serving Chief Justice of India, holding that office from 1978 to 1985?
questions.explanation: Y.V. Chandrachud served as Chief Justice of India from 1978 to 1985, the longest tenure in that office.

questions.q: The collegium system, by which judges of the higher judiciary are recommended for appointment, evolved through the Supreme Court's judgments commonly called the what?
questions.explanation: The collegium system evolved through the series of Supreme Court rulings known as the Judges' Cases.

questions.q: The seat of the Supreme Court is at Delhi, but the Constitution allows the Court to sit elsewhere with the approval of whom?
options: The Chief Justice, with the President's approval
questions.explanation: Under Article 130, the Supreme Court may sit at places other than Delhi as the Chief Justice may decide with the approval of the President.

questions.q: The Supreme Court replaced which earlier body as the apex appellate authority after the Constitution came into force?
options: The Federal Court of India and appeals to the Privy Council
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court succeeded the Federal Court of India and ended appeals to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in London.

questions.q: Under Article 141, the law declared by the Supreme Court has what character?
options: Binding on all courts within India
questions.explanation: Article 141 provides that the law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within the territory of India.
